paraphrase: Ned says that Brandon and Lyanna Stark had 'the wolf blood', making them wild and reckless; Jon shows similar impulsive, aggressive tendencies (attacking Alliser twice, a violent outburst in training) that he normally suppresses.
prior_failed_quote: "The wolf blood," Ned said calmly. "It comes down through Rickard and your line, I think. Brandon had it. Lyanna as well. I do not."
prior_note: Ned explains 'wolf blood' - wildness of Brandon and Lyanna Stark; Jon inherits similar traits and Targaryen temper

paraphrase: Parallel drawn to Daenerys's Book 5 arc: like Jon she suppresses her own desires for politics, then after her own crisis has visions reconnecting her to her Targaryen identity and embraces 'fire and blood' -- offered as a template for how crisis could similarly unleash Jon's suppressed side.
prior_failed_quote: In the beginning the world was all darkness, and in this darkness dwelt the Others. They were beings of frozen malice, and they hated all warmth, all light, all life. But the humans that dwelt upon the earth rose up and fought them, and at last defeated them, and drove them back into the far north.
prior_note: Parallel to Dany's arc: both leaders of outcasts/refugees; both magically transformed; both drawn to warrior-type partners; both embrace 'fire and blood'

paraphrase: Melisandre has a vision of Jon as 'a man', then 'a wolf', then 'a man again', hinting his spirit will live inside Ghost for a time before his human body revives.
prior_failed_quote: Now he was a man, now a wolf, now a man again. But the skulls were here as well, the skulls were all around him. Melisandre had tried to warn the boy of it. Enemies all around him, daggers in the dark. He would not listen.
prior_note: Melisandre's vision of Jon's transformation via warg-possession; hints at resurrection and spirit-in-Ghost mechanics

spoken_quote: Only death can pay for life
paraphrase: Melisandre's magical rule 'Only death can pay for life' -- resurrection/blood magic always exacts a price: Beric dies to revive Stoneheart; Mirri Maz Duur's healing of Drogo costs Daenerys's unborn child; Mirri's own death enables Daenerys's dragons to hatch.
prior_failed_quote: Only death can pay for life. It is known.
prior_note: Melisandre's blood magic principle; establishes cost of resurrection magic in the books

paraphrase: Alternative 'no-death' resurrection precedent: Thoros of Myr revives Beric repeatedly without anyone dying, but Beric loses more memory and identity each time -- suggesting the cost of Jon's resurrection could be his sense of self rather than someone else's life.
prior_failed_quote: Thoros had been in the Riverlands, red robed and drunk and half-mad on wine, preaching the gospel of the red god from the back of a stumbling horse. He had stopped at Pennytree to preach the word to the Brother's of the Faith, the warrior monks that protected the septons of the Trident as they made their pilgrimage through the land. No one had wanted to listen to his mad god, but he'd been given food and shelter nonetheless, and when he departed, a grateful septon had pressed a handful of copper coins into his palm, and told him that the red god had blessed his coming.
prior_note: Thoros reviving Beric without sacrifice; precedent for resurrectionwithout death-payment (but with memory loss)

paraphrase: Physical-transformation precedents: Catelyn's hair turns white and her eyes glow red after becoming Lady Stoneheart; Theon's hair turns white after his own symbolic death and rebirth.
prior_failed_quote: Catelyn Stark's hair had turned white. The watcher noted that her eyes remained red, raw from tears and sleepless nights, but there was a cold fury in them now. Her face was gaunt and bloodless, her thin lips twisted in an expression of pure hate.
prior_note: Stoneheart's transformation after resurrection: white hair, red eyes, lost humanity; precedent for Jon's possible post-resurrection state

paraphrase: Undead-state precedents show both perks (Beric, Coldhands, and Melisandre don't need to eat or sleep, which could help in a long war or journey) and downsides (memory loss, rotted/wounded appearance, a heart that doesn't beat).
prior_failed_quote: We have provisions enough, but our men suffer in the cold. The Lord of Light has blessed us with victory over the wildlings, but the price is heavy. We have lost good men to this cold, and many more lie maimed and crippled, unable to fight. I would have us march south, where the living men do not freeze in their beds at night, where the land itself seems to warm us with its prosperity.
prior_note: Undead/magical benefits: Beric, Coldhands, Melisandre don't need food/sleep/warmth - potential advantages for resurrected Jon

paraphrase: Melisandre claims Stannis is Azor Ahai reborn, but Jon can see that Stannis's glowing sword is not the true Lightbringer, prompting the question of who the real Azor Ahai is if not Stannis.
prior_failed_quote: The sword was singing to him, or so it seemed. It was not the ordinary ringing of steel on steel, but something richer, deeper, and more resonant. The red and gold flames were dancing now, and the shadows cast by the sword seemed to move through the hall before Jon, reaching out for the others, caressing them like long fingers. In the shadows Jon could see all the shadows of the world, and in those shadows things that had never been, things that might never be, things that had already gone away. The sword itself seemed to be made of flame, a blade of burning light. And when Jon raised the blade high and brought it crashing down, it felt like it weighed nothing, like it was an extension of his own arm. When the sword spoke, its voice was like a roar of fire.
prior_note: Jon's skepticism of Stannis/Melisandre; he doubts Stannis's Lightbringer is the true prophecied sword

paraphrase: Prophecy holds Azor Ahai will be reborn amid salt and smoke beneath a 'bleeding star', and bloody stars, smoke, and salty tears are present around the scene of Jon's death.
prior_failed_quote: The grasslands were burning as far as the eye could see. Thick plumes of smoke rose up like pillars to the sky. The coppery stench of burning bodies filled the air, and the ground beneath her was still hot to the touch. Drogo's khalasar had come through here, leaving death and ruin in their wake. Dany could see the charred remains of buildings, the bodies of men and women scattered among the ruins, twisted and blackened beyond recognition.
prior_note: Bleeding stars, smoke, salt - prophecy elements surrounding Azor Ahai context; Jon's death scene similar conditions

paraphrase: Prophecy holds Azor Ahai will be descended from Aerys, and Jon is Aerys's grandson via Rhaegar.
prior_failed_quote: The dragon has three heads. There are three fires the world needs saving from. I am not the one.
prior_note: Jon as Targaryen descended from Aerys via Rhaegar; fulfills prophecy condition of Azor Ahai descent

paraphrase: Melisandre gradually earns Jon's trust across Book 5 by making accurate predictions (e.g. correctly foreseeing three rangers would be killed with their eyes gouged out), setting up that Jon may come to accept her guidance on the Azor Ahai prophecy.
prior_failed_quote: Three rangers had gone missing beyond the Wall, and Melisandre saw their deaths in her flames. "They are dead," she said, her red eyes gleaming in the light of the fire. "I see them drowning in the snow, their faces black and twisted by fear and cold. They will be found with their eyes torn out." That was how they were found, exactly as she had described.
prior_note: Melisandre's accurate prophecies earn Jon's trust; she predicts specific details about ranger deaths

paraphrase: Counter-caution: Melisandre's guidance is repeatedly shown to be dangerous and unreliable -- it leads Stannis down a destructive path, involves burning people alive and shadow-assassination, and her religion demands human sacrifice.
prior_failed_quote: Melisandre has had us burn men alive, to wake a dragon. She burned them, and there was no dragon. There was only smoke and screaming and the smell of burning flesh. The Lord of Light demands that we sacrifice the pure and innocent to defeat the darkness. Stannis has taken Shireen, and I fear he means to use her.
prior_note: Melisandre's dangerous guidance and human sacrifice demands; contrast to Jon's moral compass

spoken_quote: whose name may not be spoken
paraphrase: The books have no confirmed singular 'Night King' villain as the show does; Melisandre attributes the white walkers to a nameless ice-god called 'the Great Other', possibly linked to a frozen tree-heart-of-power Bran senses in visions, which Jon might have to physically destroy.
prior_failed_quote: The Great Other would wake the dead and send them forth to devour the living. His name must not be spoken. To say it is to summon him. There are things of the darkness, things that worship the night and prey upon men in the dark. The white walkers are the servants of the Great Other.
prior_note: Melisandre describes the Great Other/white walkers; no singular 'Night King' in books (unlike show)

paraphrase: The legendary original Azor Ahai forged Lightbringer by sacrificing his beloved wife Nissa Nissa, setting the template that forging the 'real' Lightbringer requires killing someone the wielder loves.
prior_failed_quote: What is the life of one bastard boy against a kingdom?" she asked. For a moment Mirri's eyes seemed to burn red. "He was not a bastard." Drogo's hands clenched to fists. He could not speak the words. The magic of the fire had consumed his tongue. But he screamed, and it was a scream of such anguish and despair that it echoed across the dothrak sea and struck terror into the hearts of all who heard it.
prior_note: Lightbringer legend: original Azor Ahai forged sword by sacrificing beloved wife Nissa Nissa with his sword

paraphrase: Sacrifice-target variant: Jon could sacrifice himself instead -- stabbing himself or impaling himself on the frozen tree -- fulfilling his Book 1 fantasy of a glorious death and Qhorin Halfhand's earlier lesson that self-sacrifice is the true meaning of the Night's Watch oath.
prior_failed_quote: Above the Wall, where he had ridden with Qhorin Halfhand to scout Mance Rayder's army, Jon had seen things that filled him with wonder and with dread. The wildlings had come like a plague of locusts from the lands beyond the Wall, sweeping all before them.
prior_note: Jon's training; Qhorin's death; Jon considers self-sacrifice parallel to Night's Watch oath and Lightbringer forging

paraphrase: Suspicious pattern in older in-world legends -- the Bloodstone Emperor killing the Amethyst Empress, the Grey King killing a female dragon, the Winged Knight's child-of-the-forest wife dying in childbirth after he rode her dragon -- all involve a man gaining magic power via a woman's death, and most of these figures read as villains, not heroes, raising the possibility the ORIGINAL Azor Ahai caused the Long Night rather than ending it.
prior_failed_quote: The Bloodstone Emperor ruled for a thousand years, it was said, but his end was as terrible as his reign was long. He cast down his own wife from the Wall of Leng into the mists below. It is written that he meant to ascend to godhood by means of blood sacrifice, walking through the fires of the Great Other himself, but the magic devoured him instead and left only ash.
prior_note: Bloodstone Emperor legend: man gained magic power via woman's death but may have caused Long Night; cautionary parallel to Azor Ahai

paraphrase: Since the white walkers reportedly 'came back' after the first Long Night despite the original Azor Ahai's actions, the video argues this proves his method didn't actually work, undercutting the case for Jon to repeat it.
prior_failed_quote: The white walkers do not die easily. Obsidian can kill them, but the books say only dragonsteel can truly defeat them. In all of Westeros, there is only one dragonsteel blade that remains - Valyrian steel that has been quenched and reforged with blood magic.
prior_note: White walkers returned despite original Azor Ahai's actions; suggests his method didn't work permanently

paraphrase: The video's preferred resolution: Jon's heroism should come from his own moral choices -- the mercy he learned from Ned and showed toward Ygritte and an unnamed old man -- rather than from blood or prophecy, and he should spare Daenerys just as Ned once told Robert to spare the infant Daenerys.
prior_failed_quote: "You showed mercy to a wildling woman who would have let me bleed to death. You healed my wounds as if I were a brother of your Watch. You treated Ygritte with as much respect as you would any Northern girl. That shows the kind of man you are, and it's not a man who serves prophecy. It's a man who serves duty and honor."
prior_note: Ned's values of mercy and honor vs. prophecy-following; Jon should choose moral principles over destiny
